summary: Patrick describes his busiest customer support day, handling two issues: a customer who couldn't receive their registration key despite multiple attempts, and a CD that was never delivered. He shares his approach to handling shipping errors—absorbing the cost to maintain goodwill—and briefly notes that April sales were hurt by Easter but are recovering toward a potential new record.
key_points: Customer input errors are the leading cause of non-delivery, and support should diplomatically verify addresses rather than assign blame
Absorbing the cost of shipping mistakes is worthwhile because it builds customer goodwill and amounts to a rounding error in overall business costs
Even a tiny software business requires real customer support effort that can't always be handled in a five-minute email
Seasonal events like Easter can significantly impact sales for a small software business
money_quotes: I had not one but two issues that couldn't be taken care of in a five minute email. Oh no!

Customer error in the input field is the #1 cause of non-delivery, by far, so any time you get a report of non-delivery you should suggest in a non-confrontational way 'Could you just confirm your address for me so I can send this out?'
Yes, at my expense. Yes, that will eat up most if not all of the profit from this order.
Shipping bloopers fall into rounding errors in the greater scheme of things and you earn so much customer goodwill by addressing them promptly at cost.

My birthday is April 16th, which after adjusting for timezone issues means that I was karaoke-ing until the break of dawn when I 'should have' been telling you that sales for the first half of the month were crushed by Easter.
key_insight: When shipping errors happen in a small software business, eating the cost to make it right is trivially cheap compared to the customer goodwill it generates.
